summary:
Basil II 'Bulgaroktonos' (Bulgar-Slayer, reigned 976–1025 AD) was the greatest warrior-emperor of the [[event:3518|Byzantine Macedonian dynasty]] and the ruler under whom the Byzantine Empire reached its medieval zenith of power and territorial extent. His half-century reign transformed a realm threatened by internal revolts and Bulgar aggression into the dominant power of the eastern Mediterranean, recapturing territories in Syria, Georgia, Armenia, and the Balkans that had been lost for generations.

Basil's most famous campaign was his systematic destruction of the First Bulgarian Empire, culminating at the Battle of Kleidion (1014 AD), where Basil defeated and captured the Bulgarian army. His response was the act that earned him his epithet: he blinded 15,000 Bulgarian prisoners, leaving one eye to every hundredth man so they could lead the rest home. When the Bulgarian tsar Samuel saw his blinded army returning, he allegedly died of a heart attack two days later, and the Bulgarian state collapsed. Basil also oversaw the conversion of Kievan Rus to Christianity through his sister Anna's marriage to Vladimir I — the event that permanently oriented Russian civilization toward Orthodox Christianity. Despite his military achievements, Basil ruled with austere personal discipline, never married, and left no heirs, creating a succession crisis that contributed to the political instability of the later 11th century. His reign marked the high point of Byzantine power before the catastrophic defeat at Manzikert (1071) that opened Anatolia to Turkish settlement.
